title: Crossing out the old story; writing the new one
subtitle: A short visualisation for making the identity shift concrete.
excerpt: A brief visualisation from Day 11 of the SLEEP program. The image is simple: a page with the old sleep story written on it, and the physical act of crossing it out and writing something new in its place.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education as a companion visualisation for Day 11 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use after tapping or journalling on the sleep identity, to give the shift a felt, visual anchor.

content.text: A brief visualisation from Day 11 of the SLEEP program. The image gives the identity shift a physical form: a page, a pen, the deliberate act of crossing something out.
Sit comfortably. Close your eyes or soften your gaze. Three slow breaths.

Picture a piece of paper in front of you. On it, written in a familiar hand (maybe your own) is the sleep story you have been carrying: "I'm a terrible sleeper." "I never sleep properly." Whatever the exact words are for you.

Pick up a pen. Cross the sentence out — one slow, deliberate line through it. Not violent, just clear. The sentence is still visible under the line, and that is fine. You're not pretending it wasn't written.
Below the crossed-out line, write a new sentence. Something simpler and truer to where you are now. "I am learning to sleep." "Sleep is getting easier." "I am a person who rests." Choose what fits.
Read the new sentence. Let it sit on the page. Open your eyes when ready.
Original to homemade.education as a companion visualisation for Day 11 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
